questions.question: Define a political party and explain its main objective in a democracy.
questions.answer: A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power in the government. Their main objective is to promote policies and programmes that they believe are beneficial for society.

questions.question: Explain the three main components of a political party.
questions.answer: A political party has three components: leaders, active members, and followers. Leaders guide the party and make decisions. Active members organize activities and campaigns, while followers support the party and vote for it.

questions.question: Explain any five functions of political parties in a democracy.
questions.answer: Political parties contest elections, present policies and programmes, make laws, form governments, and play the role of opposition when not in power.

questions.question: Explain how political parties help in making laws in a country.
questions.answer: Most legislators belong to political parties. They follow party policies and decisions while debating and passing laws in the legislature.

questions.question: Explain the role of opposition parties in a democracy.
questions.answer: Opposition parties monitor government activities, criticise wrong policies, and offer alternative solutions, thereby strengthening democracy.

questions.question: Explain why political parties are considered necessary for democracy.
questions.answer: Political parties organize public opinion, present policy alternatives, and help form stable governments, making them essential for democratic functioning.

questions.question: Explain the one-party system with an example.
questions.answer: In a one-party system, only one political party is allowed to rule the country. For example, China follows a one-party system dominated by the Communist Party.

questions.question: Explain the two-party system with suitable examples.
questions.answer: In a two-party system, two major parties dominate politics and alternate in power. Examples include the United States and the United Kingdom.

questions.question: Explain the multiparty system and its advantages.
questions.answer: In a multiparty system, several parties compete for power. It provides greater representation of diverse interests in society.

questions.question: Explain how coalition governments are formed in a multiparty system.
questions.answer: When no party gets a majority, multiple parties join together to form a coalition government to run the country.

questions.question: Explain the criteria for recognition of national political parties in India.
questions.answer: A party must secure at least 6% of votes in Lok Sabha or Assembly elections in four states and win at least four Lok Sabha seats to be recognized as a national party.

questions.question: Explain the difference between national and state parties.
questions.answer: National parties operate across multiple states and influence national politics, while state parties focus mainly on regional issues within a particular state.

questions.question: Explain the major challenges faced by political parties.
questions.answer: Major challenges include lack of internal democracy, dynastic succession, the influence of money and muscle power, and lack of meaningful choices for voters.

questions.question: Explain the problem of lack of internal democracy in political parties.
questions.answer: Many political parties concentrate power among a few leaders, and ordinary members have little role in decision-making.

questions.question: Explain the issue of dynastic succession in political parties.
questions.answer: Dynastic succession occurs when leadership positions are inherited within a family rather than earned through merit.

questions.question: Explain the role of money and muscle power in elections.
questions.answer: Some parties select candidates who have financial strength or influence, which can distort democratic fairness.

questions.question: Explain the anti-defection law and its purpose.
questions.answer: The anti-defection law prevents elected representatives from changing parties after being elected, ensuring political stability.

questions.question: Explain how the Election Commission regulates political parties.
questions.answer: The Election Commission registers political parties and ensures they follow election rules and financial transparency.

questions.question: Explain how public participation can help reform political parties.
questions.answer: Citizens can demand transparency, support democratic reforms, and participate actively in political processes.

questions.question: Explain the role of political parties in shaping public opinion.
questions.answer: Political parties raise issues, organize campaigns, and influence public debates to shape people's opinions.

questions.question: Explain how political parties provide access to government machinery for citizens.
questions.answer: Party leaders often help citizens communicate with government authorities and access welfare schemes.

questions.question: Explain how political parties reflect social divisions in society.
questions.answer: Different parties represent different interests, ideologies, and social groups in a country.

questions.question: Explain the importance of coalition politics in India.
questions.answer: Coalition politics allows different parties to work together to form government, representing diverse interests.

questions.question: Explain the role of regional parties in strengthening federalism in India.
questions.answer: Regional parties represent state interests and ensure that regional issues are addressed in national politics.

questions.question: Explain how reforms can improve the functioning of political parties.
questions.answer: Reforms such as internal democracy, transparency in funding, and increased citizen participation can strengthen political parties and democracy.
